text: The surgeon begins the operation by developing a peritoneal flap, visible from the transabdominal view. An indirect hernia and weakness in the femoral area are observed.
The surgeon carefully maintains the preperitoneal flap, avoiding entry into the pre-transversalis layer, while developing the preperitoneal dissection.

The surgeon is in the visceral compartment, transitioning from the medial dissection in the parietal compartment. The intermediate fascia is being taken down, and fatty and areolar tissues are reflected towards the lateral sidewall.

The surgeon focuses on reducing the small indirect hernia by grasping the hernia sac and pulling it out of the canal in an anatomical orientation, ensuring no other tissue is extracted along with the sac.

The surgeon works towards developing the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, focusing on the intermediate fascia above the bladder. The fascia serves as protection, avoiding injury to small branches off the epigastric vessels and around the retropubic space, ensuring the bladder is safeguarded.
The surgeon dissects the femoral region to identify potential hernia spaces, revealing a small fat-containing femoral hernia. The contents are reduced to create a flat area for mesh placement.
The surgeon develops a lateral pocket and identifies the round ligament, which is then transected to facilitate laying the mesh flat in the surgical space.

The surgeon identifies the round ligament and uses an energy device to transect it, ensuring the transection occurs close to the peritoneum to minimize the risk of nerve injury near the internal ring.

The surgeon performs further retropubic dissection, ensuring at least two centimeters of contralateral dissection. The surgical field is tidied up, and the critical view is established, indicating satisfaction with the progress of the case.

The surgeon selects mid-weight polypropylene mesh to cover the myopectineal orifice (MPO), addressing a small femoral defect and a relatively small indirect hernia. The mesh is fixated with a single stitch into Cooper's ligament, visible on the right, and an additional stitch onto the lacunar ligament for femoral hernia fixation.

The surgeon performs a flap closure from lateral to medial, ensuring minimal tension on the flap. The insufflation pressure is reduced to between six and eight as the simple flap closure is completed without complications.
